customer_name: Country Critters for Long Island Inc
site_name: Country Critters for Long Island Inc

citations.narrative:
The licensee had 220 regulated animals during inspection. However, the licensee is only authorized to have up to 150
regulated animals. This puts them 70 animals over what they are allowed to have under their current license. Exceeding
the number of regulated animals doesn't ensure APHIS has reviewed and authorized the facility that they have the space
and adequate number of employees to properly care for the number of animals that are currently in their care. The
licensee must either reduce the number of regulated animals to ensure they are under the upper limit of what they are
allowed to have or they must apply for a new license that authorizes them to care for the number of animals that they
currently have on hand. Correct by 5/10/26.

The facility failed to provide appropriate space for 62 African soft-fur rats. An enclosure containing 62 African soft-fur rats
was overcrowded. I observed the rats to be crawling over each other. In addition, approximately three young rats
(approximately three weeks of age) have thinning hair/hair loss on their backs and in between their shoulder blades.
Overcrowding or a lack of space can cause malnutrition, poor body condition, debility, stress, and abnormal behavior
patterns. The African soft-fur rats must be provided an enclosure/enclosures that allow them to make normal postural and
social adjustments with adequate freedom of movement. Correct starting on 5/7/26.
Note: This was corrected prior to the end of the inspection.

Sanitation wasn’t being maintained appropriately. Seven male hedgehogs were in an enclosure that had not been cleaned
in five days. The enclosure had excreta building up. In addition, a water bottle was leaking resulting in much of the
bedding in the enclosure to be wet. Wet, dirty enclosures can contribute to the spread of disease. The enclosures must be
cleaned as often as necessary to prevent contamination of the animals contained therein and to minimize disease
hazards and to reduce odors. Correct by 5/7/26.
Note: This enclosure was cleaned prior to the end of inspection.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
End of report.
n

narrative:
2.1(b)(2)(i) 
   
Requirements and application 
The licensee had 220 regulated animals during inspection. However, the licensee is only authorized to have up to 150 
regulated animals. This puts them 70 animals over what they are allowed to have under their current license. Exceeding 
the number of regulated animals doesn't ensure APHIS has reviewed and authorized the facility that they have the space 
and adequate number of employees to properly care for the number of animals that are currently in their care. The 
licensee must either reduce the number of regulated animals to ensure they are under the upper limit of what they are 
allowed to have or they must apply for a new license that authorizes them to care for the number of animals that they 
currently have on hand. Correct by 5/10/26. 
3.128 
   
Space requirements. 
The facility failed to provide appropriate space for 62 African soft-fur rats. An enclosure containing 62 African soft-fur rats 
was overcrowded. I observed the rats to be crawling over each other. In addition, approximately three young rats 
(approximately three weeks of age) have thinning hair/hair loss on their backs and in between their shoulder blades. 
Overcrowding or a lack of space can cause malnutrition, poor body condition, debility, stress, and abnormal behavior 
patterns. The African soft-fur rats must be provided an enclosure/enclosures that allow them to make normal postural and 
social adjustments with adequate freedom of movement. Correct starting on 5/7/26. 
 
Note: This was corrected prior to the end of the inspection. 
3.131(a) 
   
Sanitation. 
Sanitation wasn’t being maintained appropriately. Seven male hedgehogs were in an enclosure that had not been cleaned 
in five days. The enclosure had excreta building up. In addition, a water bottle was leaking resulting in much of the 
bedding in the enclosure to be wet. Wet, dirty enclosures can contribute to the spread of disease. The enclosures must be 
cleaned as often as necessary to prevent contamination of the animals contained therein and to minimize disease 
hazards and to reduce odors. Correct by 5/7/26. 
 
Note: This enclosure was cleaned prior to the end of inspection.

This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative. 
 
End of report. 
 
 n
